slides.title: Securing Xtensr Labs: Choosing the Controls
slides.subtitle: Protecting Spaces: applying EK 2.3.B to Scenario 2A — match each physical vulnerability at the new lab to a control that prevents, detects, or corrects, then prioritize by severity and cost
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Lesson 2.3, Day 3 of 4. Day 1 was managerial controls, 2.3.A. Day 2 was the physical-control toolkit and how to choose a control, 2.3.B. Today we apply all of it to Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs. You're on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs, which just bought a smaller research company in town, and your job is to conduct a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab. Topic 2.2 already had you identify the vulnerabilities and assess their risk; Topic 2.3's slice of the scenario is to recommend physical security controls to mitigate those risks. So today is pure application: for each vulnerability, name a control, justify it with prevent-detect-correct, and prioritize by severity and cost. No new EKs — just the work.

slides.prompt: Walk into the new Xtensr lab as the physical security team. You see: a parking lot that lets cars roll up to the lobby glass, a side fire door that staff prop open with a chair, a server room whose rack cabinet has no lock, and an open-plan area where USB drives are freely used on shared computers.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: pick TWO of these four gaps and, for each, name the single physical control from EK 2.3.B you'd recommend and say whether it prevents, detects, or corrects the attack.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. You're the physical security team walking the new Xtensr lab, and you see four gaps: cars can roll up to the lobby glass, a fire door is propped open with a chair, the server rack has no lock, and people freely plug USB drives into shared computers. Pick any two, and for each name the one control you'd recommend from yesterday's toolkit and say whether it prevents, detects, or corrects. Hold your answer — this is exactly Scenario 2A, and we'll work all four gaps together in a minute.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 3 Guided Notes have three sections, all built on Scenario 2A. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Scenario 2A: The Vulnerability Walk
sections.description: Recap the physical security team's task at the new Xtensr lab and the gaps to mitigate, carried from Topic 2.2 (EK 2.3.B.1).

sections.title: Match Each Gap to a Control
sections.description: For each Xtensr vulnerability, recommend a control and justify prevent/detect/correct (EK 2.3.B.2–B.7).

sections.title: Build the Xtensr Recommendation Order

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.3 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Three sections today, all about Scenario 2A. Section one recaps the task: you're the physical security team assessing the new Xtensr lab, carrying the vulnerabilities you identified in Topic 2.2. Section two is the heart of the day — match each gap to a control and justify prevent, detect, or correct. Section three is the enrichment: take your recommended controls and rank them into a fundable order by severity and cost, EK 2.3.B.8. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Determine mitigation strategies for risks from physical vulnerabilities (LO 2.3.B)

slides.script: Same objective as yesterday, now applied: determine mitigation strategies for risks from physical vulnerabilities — learning objective 2.3.B. Yesterday you learned the toolkit and the reasoning; today you prove you can use them on a real scenario. By the end of class you'll have walked Scenario 2A end to end — every Xtensr vulnerability matched to a control, every control justified with prevent-detect-correct, and the whole set prioritized by severity and cost. That's the exact task the College Board built into Scenario 2A for Topic 2.3.

slides.heading: Scenario 2A: Securing Xtensr Labs
slides.subheading: The unit anchor. Topic 2.3's job: recommend physical security controls to mitigate the risks identified in Topic 2.2.
slides.context: You work on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs. Xtensr is buying a smaller research company in the same town to expand their operations. You have been tasked with conducting a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab.
details: Review the building plans and current security controls for this new lab and identify physical vulnerabilities
Assess the risk from the vulnerabilities identified
Recommend physical security controls to mitigate these vulnerabilities
Recommend the placement of monitoring equipment to detect physical security breaches
slides.footer: In Topic 2.3 we focus on the third task — recommending controls to mitigate the risks. Identifying and assessing came in Topic 2.2; monitoring placement is Topic 2.4.
slides.script: Here is Scenario 2A exactly as the College Board writes it. You work on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs. Xtensr is buying a smaller research company in the same town to expand operations, and you've been tasked with conducting a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab. The full task has four parts: review the building plans and current controls to identify physical vulnerabilities; assess the risk from those vulnerabilities; recommend physical security controls to mitigate them; and recommend placement of monitoring equipment to detect breaches. The first two you did in Topic 2.2. The fourth — monitoring placement — is Topic 2.4, next lesson. Today, Topic 2.3, is the third task: recommend physical security controls to mitigate the risks. That's where we spend the period.

slides.label: Match Each Gap to a Control
slides.script: Section one of the application. Learning objective 2.3.B applied to Scenario 2A. We take each vulnerability at the new lab and choose a control, justifying it with EK 2.3.B.1's prevent, detect, correct. Sixteen minutes.

slides.heading: Xtensr Vulnerabilities → Recommended Controls
slides.subheading: The model answer for Topic 2.3's slice of Scenario 2A. Each row: the gap, the control, its EK, and what it does.
rows: Vehicles can drive up to the lobby glass wall
Install bollards (with fencing/gates at the perimeter)
Prevent — deters/stops vehicle access
Side fire door propped open; anyone can enter
Add an access control vestibule or turnstile at the entry
Prevent — stops admitting unauthorized people
No record of who enters restricted areas
Install card readers on restricted-area doors
Detect (and prevent) — logs badges, denies unauthorized
Server rack cabinet has no lock
Lock the server cabinet (and doors/computers)
Prevent — stops devices being accessed or stolen
USB drives freely used on shared computers
Disable USB ports on those computers
Prevent — blocks malware from external drives
Power loss could drop critical research systems
Connect critical devices to a UPS; add a generator
Correct — keeps devices powered through an outage
slides.footer: AP TIP: Every recommendation names the control, its EK, AND whether it prevents, detects, or corrects (EK 2.3.B.1). A control with no prevent/detect/correct justification is an incomplete answer.
slides.script: Here's the model answer — the whole Topic 2.3 slice of Scenario 2A in one table. Read each row as a complete recommendation. Vehicles reaching the lobby glass: install bollards, with fencing and gates at the perimeter, EK 2.3.B.2 — that prevents, it deters and stops vehicle access. The propped-open side door letting anyone in: an access control vestibule or turnstile, EK 2.3.B.5 — prevents admitting unauthorized people, including by accident. No record of who enters restricted areas: card readers, EK 2.3.B.4 — that one detects, by logging which badge opened which door, and prevents, by denying unauthorized badges. The unlocked server cabinet: lock it, along with doors and computers, EK 2.3.B.3 — prevents devices being accessed or stolen. Free USB use on shared machines: disable the USB ports, EK 2.3.B.6 — prevents malware from external drives. And power loss threatening research systems: a UPS for critical devices plus a generator, EK 2.3.B.7 — that corrects, keeping devices alive through an outage. Notice every row names the control, the EK, and prevent-detect-correct. Drop any one of those three and your answer is incomplete.

slides.heading: Two Gaps, Worked in Full (EK 2.3.B.1)
slides.subheading: How the reasoning runs from adversary → control → prevent/detect/correct on Scenario 2A.

left.title: The Propped-Open Side Door
examples: Adversary move: tailgate in behind staff, or walk through the propped door unchallenged
Control: an access control vestibule or turnstile at that entry (EK 2.3.B.5)
It prevents an authorized person from admitting an unauthorized one, on purpose or by accident
Pair with security awareness training (EK 2.3.A.1) so staff stop propping the door

right.title: The Unlocked Server Cabinet
examples: Adversary move: reach the rack and remove or tamper with a drive or device
Control: a lock on the server cabinet — and on the room's door (EK 2.3.B.3)
It prevents the devices from being accessed or stolen
Card readers on the room door (EK 2.3.B.4) add a record of who entered and when

slides.footer: KEY SKILL: Strong Scenario 2A answers combine controls — a vestibule AND training for the door; a cabinet lock AND a card-reader log for the server room. Layered controls mitigate a vulnerability more completely than any single control.
slides.script: Let's run two of those gaps in full so you can see the reasoning the way the exam wants it. The propped-open side door, on the left. Adversary move: tailgate in behind staff, or just stroll through the propped door unchallenged. Control: an access control vestibule or turnstile, EK 2.3.B.5, which prevents an authorized person from admitting an unauthorized one — on purpose or by accident. And pair it with security awareness training, EK 2.3.A.1 from Day 1, so staff stop propping the door in the first place — managerial and physical together. The unlocked server cabinet, on the right. Adversary move: reach the rack and pull or tamper with a drive. Control: a lock on the cabinet and the room door, EK 2.3.B.3, which prevents devices being accessed or stolen. Add card readers on that door, EK 2.3.B.4, for a record of who went in and when. That's the key skill — layering controls, even mixing managerial and physical, mitigates a vulnerability more completely than any single control.

slides.label: Prioritize the Recommendations
slides.script: Section two. Still 2.3.B on Scenario 2A. You have controls for every gap — now rank them into a plan Xtensr can actually fund, using EK 2.3.B.8: severity and cost. Eight minutes.

slides.heading: The ‘Fix Everything At Once’ Myth
slides.misconception: A good security recommendation for Xtensr lists every possible control for every gap and installs them all immediately.
slides.whyItHappens: It feels responsible to recommend maximum protection, and listing every control looks thorough. Students forget that Xtensr has a finite budget and that EK 2.3.B.8 requires prioritization.
slides.correction: EK 2.3.B.8 says organizations prioritize risk mitigations based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations. A real Scenario 2A recommendation orders the controls: a severe, cheap fix (lock the server cabinet) comes before a low-severity, expensive one (a full perimeter generator). The deliverable is a ranked plan, not an unsorted wish list.
slides.example: At Xtensr, an unlocked cabinet holding research data is a severe risk with a cheap fix — a lock — so it's funded first. Bollards across the whole lot are costly; if vehicle ramming is a low-likelihood risk here, they can come later. Recommending both is fine; recommending them in that ORDER, justified by severity and cost, is what EK 2.3.B.8 asks for.
slides.script: Here's the Day 3 trap. It feels responsible to recommend everything and install it all now, and a giant list looks thorough. But Xtensr has a budget, and EK 2.3.B.8 says organizations prioritize mitigations by the severity of the risk and the cost of the fix. So your Scenario 2A deliverable isn't an unsorted wish list — it's a ranked plan. At Xtensr, an unlocked cabinet full of research data is severe and cheap to fix, just a lock, so it goes first. Bollards across the whole parking lot are expensive; if a vehicle ramming attack is low-likelihood here, they can wait. Recommend both, absolutely — but order them by severity and cost, and justify the order. That ordering, not the length of the list, is what scores.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.3.B)

terms.definition: The Scenario 2A task of reviewing a building's plans and current controls to identify physical vulnerabilities and recommend controls to mitigate them (EK 2.3.B.1).
A recommended control matched to a specific physical vulnerability and justified by whether it prevents, detects, or corrects the attack (EK 2.3.B.1).
Combining more than one control on a single gap — such as a vestibule plus training, or a cabinet lock plus a card-reader log — to mitigate it more completely (EK 2.3.B.5).
The control recommended for Xtensr's propped-open door; it prevents an authorized person from admitting an unauthorized person to a restricted area (EK 2.3.B.5).
Ranking which Xtensr mitigations to fund first based on the severity of the risks and the cost of the recommended mitigations (EK 2.3.B.8).
The ordered set of controls a defender hands to the organization, sequenced by severity and cost rather than listed at random (EK 2.3.B.8).

slides.script: Six terms, all framed on Scenario 2A. A physical vulnerability assessment is the whole job — review the lab, find the gaps, recommend controls, EK 2.3.B.1. A mitigation strategy is one control matched to one vulnerability and justified by prevent-detect-correct. Layered controls is the skill of stacking more than one on a single gap, EK 2.3.B.5, like a vestibule plus training. The access control vestibule is the specific recommendation for the propped door. And the budget pair: risk prioritization and the recommendation plan, EK 2.3.B.8 — ranking and ordering your controls by severity and cost. Know all six by name and EK number.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 2.3.B and Scenario 2A. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: Pick TWO Xtensr gaps not fully worked on the slides (for example, the free USB use on shared computers and the risk of power loss to research systems). For each, name the EK 2.3.B control, describe the adversary move it addresses, and state whether it prevents, detects, or corrects (cite EK numbers).

Xtensr can fund only two controls this quarter. Choose two from your recommendations, justify your choice using EK 2.3.B.8's two factors (severity and cost), and explain why you ranked them ahead of the others.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: pick two Xtensr gaps we didn't fully work on the slides — say the free USB use and the power-loss risk — and for each name the control, EK 2.3.B.6 for disabling USB ports and EK 2.3.B.7 for UPS and generators, describe the adversary move, and say prevent, detect, or correct. Question two: Xtensr can fund only two controls this quarter — pick two, and justify the choice with EK 2.3.B.8's two factors, severity and cost, explaining why those two beat the rest. Be precise and cite EKs.

Re-run the Lesson 2.3 self-check, then Exercise 5, where you build the full Scenario 2A control recommendation from scratch, and Exercise 6, where you rank the Xtensr mitigations by severity and cost. The quiz is your five-question check across both objectives; you take it tomorrow. Tomorrow is review and practice for all of Lesson 2.3, then the quiz.

slides.heading: End of Day 3
todayPoints: Applied EK 2.3.B to Scenario 2A: walked the new Xtensr lab as the physical security team
Matched each vulnerability to a control with its EK and justified prevent/detect/correct (B.2–B.7)
Practiced layering controls — and mixing managerial (2.3.A) with physical (2.3.B) — on a single gap
Prioritized the Xtensr recommendations into a fundable plan by severity and cost (B.8)
nextPoints: Review and practice across BOTH objectives, 2.3.A and 2.3.B (no new content)
Consolidate with a worked table, a cumulative Scenario 2A pass, and a vocab recap
slides.teaser: Tomorrow there's nothing new to learn — it's all about getting fast and sure. We'll sweep both objectives, run one more cumulative pass through Xtensr, and then you take the quiz.
slides.script: Day 3 done. You've applied the whole of 2.3.B to Scenario 2A — every Xtensr gap matched to a control, justified with prevent-detect-correct, and prioritized by severity and cost — and you've seen how managerial and physical controls layer together. Tomorrow is review and practice: no new EKs, just consolidating both objectives with a worked table, one more cumulative pass through Xtensr, a Stop and Think, and a vocabulary recap, and then the Lesson 2.3 quiz. Review all your Guided Notes from Days 1 through 3 tonight — managerial controls, the physical toolkit, the prevent-detect-correct reasoning, and the severity-cost prioritization.
